questions.stem: The sup norm (uniform norm) on $C[a,b]$ is defined by $\|f\|_\infty =$
questions.explanation: On $C[a,b]$ the sup norm is $\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{x \in [a,b]} |f(x)|$. Because $f$ is continuous on a compact interval the supremum is attained, so it is in fact a maximum. Convergence in this norm is uniform convergence.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: The metric on $C[a,b]$ induced by the sup norm is $d(f,g) =$
questions.explanation: The induced metric is $d(f,g) = \|f - g\|_\infty = \sup_{x \in [a,b]} |f(x) - g(x)|$. Convergence $d(f_n, f) \to 0$ is exactly uniform convergence of $f_n$ to $f$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: A fundamental structural fact about $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm is that it is:
questions.explanation: Equipped with the sup norm, $C[a,b]$ is complete: every uniformly Cauchy sequence of continuous functions converges uniformly to a continuous limit. A complete normed vector space is a Banach space, so $C[a,b]$ is a Banach space.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Convergence of a sequence $f_n$ to $f$ in the metric of $C[a,b]$ is equivalent to:
questions.explanation: Since $d(f_n, f) = \sup_x |f_n(x) - f(x)|$, the statement $d(f_n, f) \to 0$ is precisely the statement that $f_n \to f$ uniformly on $[a,b]$. The sup-norm topology is the topology of uniform convergence.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: A family $\mathcal{F}$ of functions on $[a,b]$ is equicontinuous if:
options: for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there is a single $\delta > 0$ such that $|x - y| < \delta$ implies $|f(x) - f(y)| < \varepsilon$ for all $f \in \mathcal{F}$.
questions.explanation: Equicontinuity requires one $\delta$ that works simultaneously for every member of the family: for each $\varepsilon > 0$ there is $\delta > 0$ with $|x - y| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x) - f(y)| < \varepsilon$ for all $f \in \mathcal{F}$. The $\delta$ is uniform across the family, not merely across $x$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: The Arzela-Ascoli theorem characterizes the relatively compact subsets of $C[a,b]$ as those that are:
questions.explanation: Arzela-Ascoli: a subset of $C[a,b]$ has compact closure if and only if it is uniformly bounded and equicontinuous. Equivalently, every uniformly bounded equicontinuous sequence has a uniformly convergent subsequence.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: The Stone-Weierstrass theorem (polynomial form) asserts that on $[a,b]$:
options: polynomials are dense under the $L^2$ norm but not the sup norm.
every continuous function is a polynomial.
only differentiable functions can be approximated.
polynomials are dense in $C[a,b]$ under the sup norm.
questions.explanation: The Weierstrass approximation theorem states that the polynomials are dense in $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm: every continuous function on $[a,b]$ is a uniform limit of polynomials. This is the prototypical case of the Stone-Weierstrass theorem.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Compute the distance $d(f, g) = \|f - g\|_\infty$ in $C[0,1]$ between $f(x) = x$ and $g(x) = x^2$.
questions.explanation: On $[0,1]$, $|f - g| = x - x^2$, which is maximized where its derivative $1 - 2x = 0$, i.e. at $x = 1/2$. The maximum value is $\tfrac12 - \tfrac14 = \tfrac14$. So $d(f,g) = \tfrac14$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: In an infinite-dimensional space such as $C[a,b]$, closed and bounded sets are:
questions.explanation: The Heine-Borel theorem (closed and bounded implies compact) holds in finite-dimensional spaces but fails in $C[a,b]$. The closed unit ball of $C[a,b]$ is bounded and closed yet not compact; compactness additionally requires equicontinuity by Arzela-Ascoli.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Compute $\|f\|_\infty$ in $C[0, \pi]$ for $f(x) = \sin x$.
questions.explanation: On $[0, \pi]$ the sine function attains its maximum value $1$ at $x = \pi/2$ and is nonnegative throughout. Hence $\|\sin\|_\infty = \sup_{[0,\pi]} |\sin x| = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Why is $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm complete, while $C[a,b]$ with the $L^1$ norm $\int_a^b |f|\,dx$ is not?
options: The sup norm has no Cauchy sequences.
Both norms give the same topology.
A uniformly Cauchy sequence of continuous functions has a continuous uniform limit, whereas an $L^1$-Cauchy sequence of continuous functions may converge to a discontinuous function.
The $L^1$ norm is not a norm.
questions.explanation: Uniform (sup-norm) Cauchy sequences of continuous functions converge uniformly, and the uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous, so the limit stays in the space. Under the $L^1$ norm a Cauchy sequence of continuous functions can approach a discontinuous step function, which is not in $C[a,b]$, so that space is incomplete.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Compute the distance $d(f, g) = \|f - g\|_\infty$ in $C[0, 2]$ between $f(x) = x$ and the constant $g(x) = 1$.
questions.explanation: On $[0,2]$, $|f - g| = |x - 1|$, which attains its maximum at the endpoints: $|0 - 1| = 1$ and $|2 - 1| = 1$. So $d(f, g) = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: A uniformly bounded, equicontinuous sequence $f_n$ in $C[a,b]$ is guaranteed by Arzela-Ascoli to have:
questions.explanation: Arzela-Ascoli guarantees that a uniformly bounded and equicontinuous sequence has a subsequence converging uniformly (that is, in the sup norm) to a continuous function. This is the sequential form of relative compactness in $C[a,b]$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: A family of functions with a common Lipschitz constant $K$ (so $|f(x) - f(y)| \leq K|x - y|$ for all $f$ in the family) is:
questions.explanation: A common Lipschitz bound gives equicontinuity: given $\varepsilon > 0$, choose $\delta = \varepsilon / K$; then $|x - y| < \delta$ forces $|f(x) - f(y)| \leq K|x - y| < \varepsilon$ for every $f$ in the family, with the same $\delta$. Uniformly bounded derivatives are a common source of equicontinuity.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Compute $\|f\|_\infty$ in $C[0, 2]$ for $f(x) = 3x - 1$.
questions.explanation: On $[0,2]$, $f(x) = 3x - 1$ is increasing, ranging from $f(0) = -1$ to $f(2) = 5$. Thus $|f|$ takes values from $1$ up to $5$, so $\|f\|_\infty = \max(|-1|, |5|) = 5$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: The sup norm satisfies the triangle inequality in the form:
questions.explanation: For each $x$, $|f(x) + g(x)| \leq |f(x)| + |g(x)| \leq \|f\|_\infty + \|g\|_\infty$. Taking the supremum over $x$ gives $\|f + g\|_\infty \leq \|f\|_\infty + \|g\|_\infty$. Equality can fail.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which sequence in $C[0,1]$ shows that the closed unit ball is NOT compact (it is bounded but has no uniformly convergent subsequence)?
questions.explanation: The functions $f_n(x) = \sin(n\pi x)$ all satisfy $\|f_n\|_\infty \leq 1$, so the sequence is bounded, but they oscillate ever faster and fail to be equicontinuous; no subsequence converges uniformly. This is a standard witness that the unit ball of $C[0,1]$ is not compact.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Stone-Weierstrass guarantees that a continuous function on $[a,b]$ can be uniformly approximated to within any $\varepsilon > 0$ by:
questions.explanation: By the Weierstrass approximation theorem, for any continuous $f$ on $[a,b]$ and any $\varepsilon > 0$ there is a polynomial $p$ with $\|f - p\|_\infty < \varepsilon$. The degree of $p$ may need to be large; there is no fixed-degree bound.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Compute the distance $d(f, g) = \|f - g\|_\infty$ in $C[0, 2]$ between $f(x) = x^2$ and $g(x) = 0$.
questions.explanation: On $[0,2]$, $|f - g| = x^2$ is increasing, so its supremum is attained at $x = 2$, giving $\|f - g\|_\infty = 2^2 = 4$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Pointwise boundedness plus equicontinuity of a family on a compact interval implies:
options: that every member is a polynomial.
questions.explanation: On a compact interval, an equicontinuous family that is bounded at each point is in fact uniformly bounded. A finite cover by $\delta$-intervals (from equicontinuity) plus boundedness at the finitely many centers bounds the whole family. This is part of the machinery behind Arzela-Ascoli.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Is the single sequence $f_n(x) = x^n$ on $[0,1]$ equicontinuous?
options: Equicontinuity does not apply to sequences.
No, because the functions steepen without bound near $x = 1$.
questions.explanation: The slopes $f_n'(x) = n x^{n-1}$ blow up near $x = 1$, so no single $\delta$ works for all $n$: the family is not equicontinuous. This is consistent with $x^n$ failing to converge uniformly on $[0,1]$.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which statement about $C[a,b]$ under the sup norm is correct?
options: It is an infinite-dimensional Banach space.
questions.explanation: The monomials $1, x, x^2, \ldots$ are linearly independent, so $C[a,b]$ is infinite-dimensional. It is complete under the sup norm, hence a Banach space. The sup norm does not come from an inner product, so it is not a Hilbert space.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: If $f_n \to f$ in $C[a,b]$ (sup norm) and each $f_n$ is continuous, then the limit $f$ is:
questions.explanation: Convergence in the sup norm is uniform convergence, and the uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous. So $f \in C[a,b]$. This closure property is exactly why $C[a,b]$ is complete.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which family on $[0,1]$ is equicontinuous and uniformly bounded, hence relatively compact by Arzela-Ascoli?
questions.explanation: For $g_n(x) = \sin(nx)/n$, the derivative $g_n'(x) = \cos(nx)$ is bounded by $1$, giving a common Lipschitz constant and hence equicontinuity, and $\|g_n\|_\infty \leq 1/n \leq 1$ gives uniform boundedness. The other families either fail equicontinuity ($\sin(nx)$, $x^n$) or boundedness ($nx$).
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Work in $C[0,1]$ with the sup norm, taking $f(x) = x$ and $g(x) = x^2$.
questions.solution: The distance is the supremum over $[0,1]$ of $|f(x) - g(x)| = x - x^2$. This expression is maximized at the interior point $x = 1/2$, where it attains the value {{1}}, so $d(f,g)$ equals that number. Because $C[0,1]$ with the sup norm is complete, the verdict on whether it is a Banach space is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $|f - g| = x - x^2$ has derivative $1 - 2x$, zero at $x = 1/2$, giving the maximum $\tfrac12 - \tfrac14 = \tfrac14 = 0.25$. So $d(f,g) = 0.25$. A complete normed space is a Banach space, so $C[0,1]$ qualifies.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Work in $C[0,2]$ with the sup norm, taking $f(x) = 2x$ and the constant $g(x) = 3$.
questions.solution: The distance is $\sup_{[0,2]} |2x - 3|$. Checking the endpoints, $|2(0) - 3| = 3$ and $|2(2) - 3| = 1$, and the function $|2x-3|$ is largest at an endpoint, so the distance $d(f,g)$ equals {{1}}. Since the sup norm topology is the topology of uniform convergence, convergence in this metric is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $|2x - 3|$ on $[0,2]$ takes value $3$ at $x = 0$ and $1$ at $x = 2$; it is piecewise linear with the larger endpoint value at $x = 0$, so the supremum is $3$. Convergence in the sup norm is uniform convergence.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Consider the family $g_n(x) = \dfrac{\sin(nx)}{n}$ on $[0,1]$, and recall the Arzela-Ascoli theorem.
questions.solution: Each $g_n$ has derivative $\cos(nx)$, bounded in absolute value by the constant {{1}}, so the whole family shares a common Lipschitz constant and is therefore equicontinuous. Combined with uniform boundedness, Arzela-Ascoli says the family has a uniformly convergent subsequence, so whether the family is relatively compact is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $g_n'(x) = \cos(nx)$ satisfies $|\cos(nx)| \leq 1$, so $1$ is a common Lipschitz constant, giving equicontinuity. With $\|g_n\|_\infty \leq 1/n \leq 1$ the family is uniformly bounded, so by Arzela-Ascoli it is relatively compact.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis

questions.stem: Consider $f(x) = x^2$ as an element of $C[-1, 3]$ with the sup norm.
questions.solution: The norm is the supremum of $x^2$ over $[-1,3]$. Comparing the values at the candidate points, the squared function is largest at $x = 3$, so the norm $\|f\|_\infty$ equals {{1}}. By the Weierstrass approximation theorem, whether $f$ (already a polynomial) can be uniformly approximated by polynomials is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: On $[-1,3]$, $x^2$ ranges from $0$ to $9$, with the maximum $9$ at $x = 3$ (exceeding the value $1$ at $x = -1$). So $\|f\|_\infty = 9$. Polynomials are dense in $C[-1,3]$, and a polynomial is trivially a uniform limit of polynomials.
questions.source: in the style of Bartle, The Elements of Real Analysis
